coredata.dc:title: Dynamic optical information encoding strategy of flexible thin films based on Ba2SiO4 phosphor for ultra-long storage time

coredata.dc:creator: Song, Yaqi
Zhang, Yanjie
Wang, Yuxin
Wu, Meng
Yang, Shiwei
Song, Jiawei
Zhao, Herui
Yu, Jingjie
coredata.dc:description: Nowadays, state-of-the-art for optical data storage (ODS) have emerged as a leader in the competition of data storage technologies. However, the development of optical storage technology based on optically stimulated luminescence (OSL) encounter the serious problems, of single storage mode and easily decoding. In this study, a series of Ba2SiO4 (denoted as BS): Eu2+, Ln3+ (Ln = Dy, Ho) phosphors with adjustable trap distributions (trap depths of 0.6–0.8 eV) have been developed by high-temperature solid-phase method and exhibit intense green emission peak located at 504 nm under excitation of 360 nm. Typically, BS: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+ phosphor owns a shallow trap depth of 0.696 eV and BS: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Dy3+ phosphor shows a deep trap depth of 0.81 eV. Interestingly, the flexible optical information storage film prepared by BS: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+ phosphor through spin-coating technique can storage the “National Water Conservation Logo” pattern under NUV irradiation and reappear even after more than 60 days at 75 °C. Subsequently, multimodal anti-counterfeiting strategy is proposed by BS: Eu2+, Ln3+ (Ln = Dy, Ho) phosphors with adjustable trap depths and the flexible information coding device with high transmittance has been developed, in which encrypted message of “HELLO” and “86068” can be recognized at 75 °C and 130 °C, respectively. This work not only lays the foundation for realizing the durability of optical information storage, but also provides high security with multiple levels of retrievability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicates
Optical data storage
Anti-counterfeiting
Encryption
Durability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Ba2SiO4: xEu2+, yLn3+ (Ln = Dy, Ho) phosphors
Spin-Coating Technology for flexible luminescent films
Screen-printing technology for optical information security devices
PL and optical information storage properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ln3+ (Ln = Dy, Ho) phosphors and the standard pattern of Ba2SiO4 crystal (PDF#26–1403). (b) Crystal structure of Ba2SiO4 along axis and coordination geometry around Ba and Si atoms.
(a) SEM images of Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+ microcrystalline particles. (b) EDS spectra of Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+. (c–f) Elemental mapping of Ba, Si, O and Eu elements in Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+ samples.
PL spectra of Ba2SiO4: xEu2+ (x = 0.005–0.03) phosphors. (a) the excitation at 360 nm. (b) Excitation spectrum at emission peak 504 nm.
PL spectrum of Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ln3+ (Ln = Dy, Ho) phosphor. (a) Emission spectrum at 284 nm excitation. (b) Excitation spectrum at 504 nm.
Decay curves of (a) Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+ (b) Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Dy3+ (c) Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+.
Normalized TL curves of Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Dy3+ and Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+.
Schematic illustration of the LPL and PSL mechanism of the Ba2SiO4: Eu2+, Ho3+ phosphor.

Ba2SiO4: xEu2+, Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ln3+ (x = 0.005, 0.01, 0.02 and 0.03, Ln=Dy, Ho, from left to right). (a) phosphor irradiated at 254 nm for 3 min. (b) afterglow after withdrawing the UV. (c) heated at different temperatures respectively.

(a) Flexible PDMS emitters prepared with Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+ and PDMS. (b) The film is covered with a “National Water Conservation Logo” pattern mask. (c) “National Water Conservation Logo” pattern reading process.

Ba2SiO4-based cyan optical information encoding devices and their optical information storage process. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Optical message encryption and decoding. (a) “Vehicles Allowed” sign printed on the PDMS film. (b) “HELLO” message printed on the PDMS film superimposed on the digits with the “86068” message.

head.ce:title: Dynamic optical information encoding strategy of flexible thin films based on Ba2SiO4 phosphor for ultra-long storage time

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute of Photonics, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian, 116034, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Institute of Photonics, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian, 116034, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nowadays, state-of-the-art for optical data storage (ODS) have emerged as a leader in the competition of data storage technologies. However, the development of optical storage technology based on optically stimulated luminescence (OSL) encounter the serious problems, of single storage mode and easily decoding. In this study, a series of Ba2SiO4 (denoted as BS): Eu2+, Ln3+ (Ln = Dy, Ho) phosphors with adjustable trap distributions (trap depths of 0.6–0.8 eV) have been developed by high-temperature solid-phase method and exhibit intense green emission peak located at 504 nm under excitation of 360 nm. Typically, BS: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+ phosphor owns a shallow trap depth of 0.696 eV and BS: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Dy3+ phosphor shows a deep trap depth of 0.81 eV. Interestingly, the flexible optical information storage film prepared by BS: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+ phosphor through spin-coating technique can storage the “National Water Conservation Logo” pattern under NUV irradiation and reappear even after more than 60 days at 75 °C. Subsequently, multimodal anti-counterfeiting strategy is proposed by BS: Eu2+, Ln3+ (Ln = Dy, Ho) phosphors with adjustable trap depths and the flexible information coding device with high transmittance has been developed, in which encrypted message of “HELLO” and “86068” can be recognized at 75 °C and 130 °C, respectively. This work not only lays the foundation for realizing the durability of optical information storage, but also provides high security with multiple levels of retrievability.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, optical information storage based on luminescent materials has attracted much attention due to its fast response speed, high storage density, low cost, and high security [1–9]. Long afterglow materials are commonly used in the field of optical information coding technology because of their excellent properties of luminescence (PersL), thermoluminescence (TL), photostimulated luminescence (PSL) and mechanoluminescence (ML), which can absorb photon energy and store it in traps under certain light stimuli, and then gradually release the trapped photons to generate luminescence emission under thermal or infrared stimulation with the duration of several seconds to hour [10–16].

Further, various of novel anti-counterfeiting materials with rich optical properties have been developed by taking advantage of the visual identification of fluorescent materials, bright luminescent colors and excellent luminescent properties [17–19]. Unfortunately, the current long afterglow materials suffer from the problems of, single excitation modes and short storage times (generally between 2 and 15 days), which hardly meet the requirement of anti-counterfeiting application [20–26]. Until now, it is still a major challenge to develop luminescent materials with multi-stimulation mode and long storage times (more than 60 days), for anti-counterfeiting application with high safety level.

Compared with their counterparts such as aluminates and phosphates, silicates have been regarded as an ideal matrix for long afterglow luminescent materials due to its abundant raw materials, simple preparation method, adaptability, stable crystal structure, thermal stability and chemical stability [27–29]. Alkaline earth ortho-silicates, such as ABSiO4: Eu2+ (A, B = Ca, Sr, Ba), have attracted much attention and are regarded as an important class of silicate materials. The luminescence properties of some rare earth ion-doped phosphors and the LPL properties of Sr2SiO4: Eu2+, Ln3+ have been studied. However, the design strategy of strongly enhanced LPL of Ba2SiO4: Eu2+, Ho3+ guided by trap distribution modulation via appropriate Ln3+ co-doping and the PSL for optical information storage have not been studied [30–33]. Wang et al. reported the long afterglow phosphor Ba2SiO4: Eu2+, Ho3+. They found that it had remarkable long afterglow luminescence performance [34]. This was mainly due to the introduction of Ho3+ co-doping during the preparation process, which adjusted the internal trap distribution of the phosphor and enhanced the afterglow luminescence. The Ba2SiO4: Eu2+, Ho3+ phosphors were fabricated into optical information storage films, which provide a broad application prospect for optical information storage. Unfortunately, the film can no longer read the target information after 15 days of excitation at 980 nm. It is obvious that the preparation of thin films with long storage time is important in the field of optical information storage.

Following the doubly doped information storage film, a novel multidimensional flexible media optical storage technology was reported in 2019. Li et al. innovatively selected a variety of trivalent rare-earth ions co-doped into Ba2SiO4: Eu2+, which was capable of erasable multilevel optical information (patterns, numbers) entry and extraction [35]. Different trap depths are obtained by adding Tm3+, Ho3+ and Dy3+, which makes it possible to store different optical information separately in multiple traps [36–38]. However, the shortcomings of this method is that each pattern needs to be entered, the process is cumbersome and difficult to read, if we can use screen printing technology can greatly improve the regiment's admission and reading.

In order to improve the security of storing optical information storage, Pan et al. innovatively proposed a multilevel intensity optical storage scheme, mainly demonstrating the optical storage of grayscale images and multilevel data, in which Ba2SiO4: Eu2+, Ho3+ fluorescent thin films were used as the storage medium. The stored information was converted into a mosaic pattern and passed through a mask for ultraviolet irradiation. The luminescence response was linearly proportional to the excitation power. The bit error rate (BER) of optical data storage with this method was less than 1.6%, which was a strong evidence of the significant progress in optical information storage [39]. The process of converting encrypted information into a binary mosaic pattern was more cumbersome, while the storage essence was still covered using a mask.

In this work, a series of rare earth doped Ba2SiO4 (BS) phosphors with pure phase have been by high-temperature solid-phase method. We first prepared Eu2+ single-doped phosphor to obtain a strong green emission at 504 nm under 360 nm excitation. Subsequently, different trap depths were realized by Ln3+ (Dy, Ho) co-doping, which is more conducive to the realization of multi-mode optical information storage. Flexible luminescence layers were prepared by spin-coating BS: Eu2+, Ho3+ and optical information storage devices (OISD) were prepared by screen-printing BS: Eu2+, BS: Eu2+, Ln3+ (Ln = Dy, Ho). Through the fabrication of Ba2SiO4: Eu2+, Ho3+ fluorescent films with the trap depth of 0.696 eV, patterned information can be stored after passing UV light through a photomask, and then the stored information retrieved by thermal stimulation. After emptying the internal electrons, the stimulation was performed again and still showed excellent information reading function, which reflected the repeated reading function of information and improved the utilization rate and application value. Surprisingly, the films are still able to read out the target pattern after 60 days, which offers the prospect of long-term optical information storage. Moreover, the flexible optical information coding devices prepared by screen-printing technology and could be read by light stimulation, thermal stimulation, and water bath. This optical information storage method had outstanding advantages such as fast speed, convenience, and long-time storage without power consumption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Ba2SiO4: xEu2+, yLn3+ (Ln = Dy, Ho) phosphors
ce:section.ce:para: A series of Ba2SiO4: xEu2+ (x = 0.005–0.03) phosphors were prepared by a two-step high-temperature solid-state reaction method using BaCO3 (99.9%, Sinopharm), SiO2 (Sinopharm), and Eu2O3 (99.99%, Aldrich) as raw materials. First, BaCO3 and SiO2 were weighed according to the calculated stoichiometric ratios, and second, Eu2O3 and Ln2O3 (Ln = Dy, Ho) trap centers were added to prepare persistent luminescent phosphors for multilevel optical data storage. After weighing, raw materials were ground with agate mortar for 30 min, mixed well and placed in a molybdenum crucible, and finally sintered in a box muffle furnace at 1000 °C for 6 h in air and cooled at room temperature and removed from the mill. The mixtures were subsequently put in a tube furnace and sintered at 1320 °C for 4 h under a 3% H2/N2 atmosphere (100 mL/min). The samples were cooled to room temperature and then ground again to obtain the final products for subsequent characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spin-Coating Technology for flexible luminescent films
ce:section.ce:para: The core element of the Spin-Coating Technology is the preparation of an elastic substrate for the luminescent film using polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) (Sylgard 184, Dow Corning), followed by the mixing of the powdered luminescent material with an organic precursor. Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+ phosphor sieved through a 300-mesh sieve, weighed the mass ratio of 3: 7 PDMS (PDMS consists of the main body and the curing agent, the main body and the curing agent of the mixing ratio of 10: 1), homogeneous mixing to get the fluorescent slurry, and leave it to eliminate bubbles for a period of time. Then, the fluorescent paste was selected onto a smooth stainless steel or glass substrate by spinning coating technology, and was cured at 80 °C for 2 h or at room temperature for 24 h, and then gently removed with tweezers to obtain Ba2SiO4 flexible fluorescent film or Ba2SiO4-based elastomer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Screen-printing technology for optical information security devices
ce:section.ce:para: First, Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Dy3+ and Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+ phosphors (sieved through 300 mesh) with a 1: 1: 1 wt ratio were mixed with PDMS or a silica gel (in liquid) in a weight ratio of 3: 7. Then, the prepared fluorescent layer was printed on the glass substrate or elastic substrate through the designed pattern, and the final printed device was put into the oven at 100 °C for 20 min. And we further analyzed the luminescence of the layers under natural light (NL mode) and UV irradiation (PL mode), after removal of the UV source after 5 min irradiation (PersL), after heating to different temperatures after the afterglow had disappeared (TSL), and at the later stages of heating (TSPL).

Flexible information storage film: The flexible light-emitting film is covered with a specific pattern mask, stimulated by a UV lamp (254 nm) for 5 min, then the light source and the mask are removed, at this time, the specific pattern of information has been recorded in the light-emitting film, and finally the flexible film will be heated to 85 °C, the target information could be read out.

Optical information storage device (OISD): After irradiation with a 254 nm UV lamp for 5 min, the device with Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+ was placed in hot water at 75 °C to read “Allow to travel”, and Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Dy3+ read “86068” at 130 °C.

Ba2SiO4 structural simulation was performed by the Diamond software using Ba2SiO4 crystal structure as a starting model. The phase structure of the phosphors was tested through the Powder X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu; XRD-7000S) operated at 40 kV and 30 mA. The data were collected with the step of 0.02° (2θ) and the counting time of 0.2 s per step in the mode of continuous scanning. Characterization of phosphor particle morphology was performed by field emission electron microscope (JSM-7800F, JEOL). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured at room temperature by the Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a xenon lamp at a nominal power of 150 W. The slits of emission and excitation were both set to 1.0 nm. The luminescence lifetime of Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ln3+ (Ln = Dy, Ho) phosphors was measured by a spectrofluorometer (Hitachi F-7000). The thermoluminescence (TL) curve was detected by the TOSL-3DS model instrument. The temperature range was room temperature - 200 °C, the heating rate was 1 °C/s, and the sample was irradiated by ultraviolet light for 5 min before the test.

The physical phases and crystal structures of high-temperature solid-state reaction Ba2SiO4: xEu2+, Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, yDy3+ and Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, yHo3+ (x, y = 0.005–0.03) phosphors were analyzed by XRD. Fig. 1 a presents the typical XRD patterns of Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Dy3+ and Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+ samples with standard data of Ba2SiO4 crystal (PDF#26–1403). As shown in Fig. 1a, the main phase peaks of all samples are in good agreement with the standard card, and no other impurity phase is detected, indicating that Ba2SiO4 doped with Eu2+, Dy3+ and Ho3+ ions are in a pure phase. The samples doped with Eu2+, Dy3+ and Ho3+ ions all crystallized in the space group pmcn (No. 62), with no significant effect on the crystal structure.

Fig. S1 illustrates XRD patterns of Ba2SiO4: xEu2+, Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, yDy3+ and Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, yHo3+ (x, y = 0.005–0.03) samples using the standard XRD pattern of Ba2SiO4 as a reference (PDF#26–1403). XRD patterns of Ba2SiO4: xEu3+ (x = 0.005–0.03) phosphors and the corresponding diffraction standard data of Ba2SiO4 crystal (PDF#26–1403) are displayed in Fig. S1a. It can be found that the diffraction peaks of the phosphors XRD spectra with different Eu2+ concentrations are very consistent with those of Ba2SiO4 (PDF#26–1403), and increasing the concentration of Eu2+ does not cause a shift of the diffraction peaks and therefore does not lead to any significant change in the crystalline phase of the host Ba2SiO4. Fig. S1b shows the XRD patterns of as-prepared samples of Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, yDy3+ (y = 0.005–0.03). All the diffraction peaks match well with the standard data for (PDF#26–1403), indicating that the doping of Eu2+ and Dy3+ into the host lattice has no signification influence on the host crystal structure. In Fig. S1c, the XRD patterns of Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, yHo3+ (y = 0.005–0.03) samples match well with standard data (PDF#26–1403) of Ba2SiO4 crystal. All samples doped with Eu2+ and Ho3+ ions correspond to the orthorhombic crystal lattice with the space group of pmcn (62). Clearly, no other impurity phases (e.g. Eu2O3 and Ho2O3) can be found, which indicates that Eu2+ and Ho3+ ions can be effectively incorporated into Ba2SiO4 host lattice.

Fig. 1b explains the crystal structure of Ba2SiO4 along c axis and the coordination environment around the Ba and Si atoms. The skeleton of BS was constructed by the Si–O tetrahedron and two different kinds of Ba–O polyhedrons with the Wyckoff position (Wyckoff Positions were used to represent the symmetry of equivalent atoms in a crystal cell) 4c, i.e., 10-coordinated Ba1 and 9-coordinated Ba2. The Ba2SiO4 owns a quartet structure with a = 7.51 Å, b = 10.21 Å, c = 5.81 Å, and Z = 4. In the lattice, the 4c position is occupied by two Ba1 and Ba2, because the ionic radii of Ba1 and Ba2 are similar to those of Eu2+, Dy3+ and Ho3+ therefore more suitable for replacing Ba.

To observe the morphology and elemental composition, the microscopic morphology of the Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+ powder samples was observed using scanning electron microscope. Fig. 2 a shows the typical morphology of the Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+ sample observed under SEM, which consists of irregular particles of varying sizes resembling spherical shapes, which are considered to be typical silicate particles. The corresponding energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping was also employed to further investigate the distribution of Ba, Si, O and Eu elements for Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+ composites as shown in Fig. 2b. It can be seen from Fig. 2c–f that the elements of Ba, Si, O and Eu are uniformly dispersed in the Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+ particle. Moreover, no obvious element aggregation induced by the phase separation or impurity was observed, which is consistent with the XRD patterns.

Analysis of SEM patterns further revealed that doping in a three-dimensional mesh structure containing specific anionic tetrahedral groups XO4 (X = P, Ge, Si …) transition metal or rare earth ions in the high valence state, it was possible to realize the transition of ions from the high valence state to the high valence state under air atmosphere The spontaneous reduction of ions from high to low valence states can be realized under air atmosphere [43–48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL and optical information storage properties
ce:section.ce:para: The photoluminescent emission (PL) and excitation (PLE) spectra of Ba2SiO4: xEu2+ (x = 0.005–0.03) are shown in Fig. 3 . In Fig. 3a, Ba2SiO4: xEu2+ (x = 0.005–0.03) phosphors exhibit the intense green emission peak at 504 nm under excitation of 360 nm, attributed to the 5d-4f transition of Eu2+ ions in host lattice as the luminescence center. It can be also found from the inset that the intensity of the emission peak at 504 nm firstly increases and then decreases along with the increasing of Eu2+ concentration in the host, until reaching the maximum at x = 0.02.

As could be seen from Fig. 3b, the PLE spectrum showed an asymmetric wide excitation band covering from 260 nm to 475 nm when monitored the emission peak at 504 nm corresponding to the 4f ground state to the 5d excited state of Eu2+. It covers the ultraviolet region and is expected to be an ideal blue phosphor for near-ultraviolet excited white LEDs.

The PL and PLE spectra of all the as-obtained samples BS: 0.02Eu2+, yLn3+ (y = 0.005–0.03) were shown in Fig. S2 a-d. For sample BS: Eu2+, yLn3+ (y = 0.005–0.03), while the emission band between 450 and 600 nm was ascribable to the 5d-4f electronic dipole-allowed transitions of Eu2+. At 504 nm, the excitation spectrum showed a broad band from 260 to 470 nm. As shown in Fig. S2a, upon increasing the concentration of Dy3+ ions, the intensity of the emission peak first increases and then decreases, reaching a maximum at y = 0.02. Fig. S2c showed that the luminescence intensity also increases and then decreases with the increase of Ho3+ concentration, and the position of the emission peak does not move significantly.

Nevertheless, based on the emission spectra of Fig. 4 a, it could be seen that the position of the emission peak located at 504 nm under excitation at 360 nm, the luminescence intensity of the Dy3+ and Ho3+ doped samples was lower than that of the Eu2+-doped samples alone under the excitation at 284 nm. The afterglow phenomenon arises as a result of the decrease in luminescence intensity due to the doping of the captured ions, while the electrons are externally stimulated from the capture energy level to return to the excited state through the conduction band and release energy in the form of light on their way back to the ground state, thus compensating for the decrease in luminescence intensity brought about by the thermal outburst [42].

The PLE spectra of Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ln3+ (Ln = Dy, Ho) were shown in Fig. 4b. Monitored at 504 nm, the PLE spectra did not change significantly, showing asymmetric broad excitation bands with peaks at 284 nm and 363 nm, respectively.

To further investigate the fluorescence lifetime of Ba2SiO4 phosphors, the decay curves of Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ln3+ (Ln = Dy, Ho) phosphors excited at 254 nm and monitored at 504 nm were shown in Fig. 5 a–c. The decay curve could be fitted with the following double exponential Equation (1) [40]. Where t is the time, I
0 is the initial luminescence intensity, I
(t) is the fluorescence intensity when the decay time is t, A
1 and A
2 are a set of constants here, and τ
1 and τ
2 stand for the meanings of two kinds of decay lifetimes, fast and slow. The average decay time (τ*) can be further calculated using Equation (2) [41].
(1) Ι(t) = Ι0+ A 1exp(−t∕τ 1) + A 2exp(−t∕τ 2)
(2)
τ∗ = (A 1 τ 1 2+A 2 τ 2 2) ∕ (A 1 τ 1+A 2 τ 2)

Based on Eq. (1) and (2), the lifetime values of the phosphors are determined to be 0.123 μs, 0.579 μs, and 0.667 μs for BS: 0.02Eu2+, BS: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Dy3+ and BS: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+, respectively. By comparing the decay curves of the samples Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ln3+ (Ln = Dy, Ho), it is shown that the long afterglow properties of Ba2SiO4: Eu2+ are improved to a great extent by co-doping with Dy3+, Ho3+. After several attempts by Ln3+ co-doping, we found that Dy3+ and Ho3+ are the most favorable ion type to promote the LPL characteristics. Here, we choose Dy3+ and Ho3+ as co-dopants to regulate the trap distribution to design an excellent phosphor with brighter and longer afterglow.

In order to gain more insight into the trap distribution as well as the trapping and releasing behavior of electrons by the traps, the thermoluminescence (TL) curves of the sample Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ln3+ (Ln = Dy, Ho), which was the main method of measuring the trap effect and its role in the luminescence properties of phosphors, were tested at different temperatures (room temperature - 200 °C), as shown in Fig. 6 . For long afterglow phosphors, the presence or absence of traps within the material is fundamental to the realization of optical information storage. The energy stored in the trap inside the phosphor could be released in the form of light during the thermal stimulation heating process. By monitoring the peak value of the trap spectrum, the trap depth could be calculated using the formula. The role of doped Ln3+ in this process was to provide trap centers and Eu2+ were luminescent centers, and these traps could capture a large number of excited electrons. The trapped electrons could not escape immediately, leading to a decrease in the emission intensity. This suggests that the co-doping of Ln3+ ions modulates the distribution of traps within the material, thus enabling optical information storage.

The TL curve of Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+ was measured after 5 min pre-irradiation with ultraviolet light. The TL curve had two strong emission peaks at 74 °C and 148 °C, indicating that there were at least two types of traps in Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+ (0.694 eV and 0.842 eV respectively). It was obvious from the TL spectra that the area enclosed by the intensities and curves of the Dy3+ and Ho3+ doped samples was much higher than that of the singly doped Eu2+. The TL intensity and the enclosed area respond to the number of electron traps in the long afterglow material, and thus it could be assumed that the number of electron traps in this phosphor had been significantly increased, and thus the number of electrons returning to the ground state per unit time will increase and the afterglow time will be prolonged.

In addition, the trap depth E could be approximated by formula E = Tm/500 to obtain two emission peaks observed at 74 °C and 148 °C, and the corresponding trap depths were calculated as 0.694 eV and 0.842 eV, respectively. Where E (eV) means the depth of the trap, Tm (K) means the peak temperature of the TL curve. The emission peak is observed when the heating temperature reaches 132 °C, which the trap depth is 0.81 eV for Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Dy3+ phosphors. Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+ phosphor observed a strong emission peak at 75 °C, and the corresponding trap depth was calculated as 0.696 eV. Due to nonequivalent substitution of Ba2+ by Ho3+, an excess of positive charge must be compensated. One possible way to fulfill the charge compensation is that two Ho3+ ions replace three Ba2+ ions. In this process, large numbers of new traps are created. Comprehensively, Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+ phosphors can achieve the strongest TL intensity and appropriate trap distribution, thereby achieving the best LPL performance. This indicated that the phosphor film was a suitable medium for optical storage, which was capable of storing information for a long time at room temperature and releasing information under appropriate thermal stimulation. TL characterization clearly showed that the optical information could be stored in the three fluorescent materials selected in this paper, and fully released under thermal stimulation above 150 °C.

According to the TL analysis, the single Eu2+-doped sample has two trap centers at 74 °C (0.694 eV) and 148 °C (0.842 eV), respectively, while the Eu2+ and Ho3+ co-doped samples had a rather broad distribution of continuum trap depths at the bottom of the CB. In Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+ phosphors, Eu2+ is the luminescent center, while Ho3+ is the trap center, where Eu2+ ions exhibit 4f-5d transitions. Due to the presence of a capture center (Ho3+ ions), electrons in the conduction band (CB) can be captured by the capture center. The captured electrons will be released to CB and then migrate to the 5d state, achieving corresponding emission under thermal or light stimulation.

Based on the basis of the above results and discussion, a possible mechanism of LPL and PSL phenomena could be proposed, as illustrated in Fig. 7 . Under 254 nm UV lamp excitation, the electrons located at the ground state of Eu2+ would be promoted to higher to 5d excited states (process 1). Then electron transitions occur from the 5d level to the lower 4f level followed by the characteristic emission of Eu2+ ions as luminescence (process 2). In the meantime, a small amount of electrons could shift freely in the CB and be captured by the traps with different depths (processes 3 and 5). After switching off the UV light source, with the assistance of thermal disturbances, the electrons captured could easily escape from shallow traps to the CB directly and transfer to the activator Eu2+. This process corresponds rapid decay and generates an intense persistent luminescence in the initial stage (processes 4 and 6). After a few hours, shallow traps were depleted and fewer electrons were released through the CB, while electrons captured by deep traps could gradually leap over the shallow traps, resulting in a constant and weak luminescence. By applying an external high-temperature thermal stimulus (process 7), the trapped electrons could be rapidly released into the CB, eventually displaying the stored information in the form of photon emission.

The afterglow properties and thermally stimulated luminescence properties of Eu2+ mono-doped Ba2SiO4 samples and Eu2+/Ln3+ co-doped Ba2SiO4 samples were compared in detail. Fig. 8 a shows the Ba2SiO4 samples doped with different Eu2+ ions with concentrations of 0.005, 0.01, 0.02 and 0.03 from left to right, and Fig. 8b–c show the Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+ doped with Dy3+ and Ho3+ concentrations of 0.005, 0.01, 0.02 and 0.03, respectively. All samples were pre-irradiated under UV light for 5 min. The UV light was turned off and recorded in the dark, after the afterglow had completely disappeared the samples were heated to 75 °C, 150 °C and 75 °C respectively.

The Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+ phosphor had excellent persistent luminescence and thermostimulation properties, which could be used for optical information storage. As a result, Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+ phosphor with better optical information storage performance was selected, and a flexible optical information storage fluorescent film was prepared by using polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) as a flexible substrate. As shown in Fig. 9 a, the luminous film has a size of 2.0 × 2.0 cm and a thickness of ∼0.5 mm, and has excellent bending properties.

Fig. 9 shown the temperature-triggered optical information storage process of the fluorescent film. After covering the photomask with the skeletonized “National Water Conservation Logo” pattern on the fluorescent film, this film was exposed to UV light to record the “National Water Conservation Logo” information. When the UV light disappears, the “National Water Conservation Logo” pattern becomes invisible. The film with the specific information was then placed on a heated table at 75 °C. The “National Water Conservation Logo” pattern appeared and the information was stored. The erasable flexible thin film information storage duration was up to more than fifty days for the first time in the reported literature.

As shown in Fig. 10 , the two Arabic numerals of the number “88” were printed on coverslips with the same number of layers using Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+ as a paste using the screen-printing technique. The number “88” had the same body color under natural light. The Arabic numeral “66” was printed using Ba2SiO4: 0.0Eu2+, 0.02Dy3+ and Ba2SiO4: 0.0Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+ as pastes 3 and 4. All numbers were printed with the same number of layers. In natural light and the same UV light source irradiation of all figures showed the same body color and consistent green light emission, the naked eye could not distinguish the difference between the two kinds of paste.

Due to the fact that Dy3+, and Ho3+ are the capture centers in Ba2SiO4 phosphor, their introduction can improve the electron capture behavior of the capture centers. Therefore, after turning off the UV light source, the devices were heated to 150 °C and 75 °C respectively, and the heating number “66” showed an obvious afterglow intensity higher than that of the number “88”.

Based on the basis of the above research content, a series of green light optical information thin film devices were prepared by using Ba2SiO4-based phosphor and screen-printing technology. Two types of fluorescent slurry were used in this part, namely Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Dy3+ (denoted as paste 1) and Ba2SiO4: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+ (denoted as paste 2) Phosphor mixed with PDMS preparation. The fluorescent powder screened with a 300 mesh screen was mixed with PDMS at a mass ratio of 3: 7, and was continuously stirred for about 5 min to fully mix into a fluorescent paste. The fluorescent paste was placed at room temperature for some time to remove bubbles. The prepared fluorescent paste was printed on the flexible film below through the screen plate with a specific pattern, and then cured at 100 °C for 30 min to obtain the corresponding optical information encoding thin film device.

Flexible information coding devices with high transmittance of “Allow cars to pass” and “86068” made by screen printing technology with pastes 1 and 2 is shown in Fig. 11 . Paste 1 was used to print the circle of “Allow to travel” and “86068”, and paste 2 was used to print “Car” and “HELLO” respectively. Because pastes 1, 2 could be different temperatures of thermal stimulation for excitation, the first UV lamp irradiation was “Allow cars to pass” and “86068”, withdraw the UV lamp will be placed into the device 75 °C hot water, Ho3+ internal traps were stimulated, the “Allow to travel” circle and “86068”. The patterns of “Allow cars to pass” and “86068” were obtained, and then put into the high-temperature heating table at 130 °C, all the traps of Dy3+ and Ho3+ were excited to obtain the same patterns as those irradiated by the UV lamp. The information can be read out several times by natural light, UV light, water bath mode, and high and low-temperature heating mode.

In summary, a series of Ba2SiO4: Eu2+ phosphors and Ba2SiO4: Eu2+, Ln3+ (Ln = Dy, Ho) phosphors were prepared using a two-step method. On the basis of determining that the optimum concentration of single-doped Eu2+ was 0.02, it was experimentally derived that the concentrations of introduced Ln3+ (Ln = Dy, Ho) co-doped with Eu2+ were all 0.02. The shallow trap depth of BS: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Ho3+ phosphor is 0.696 eV, while the deep trap depth of BS: 0.02Eu2+, 0.02Dy3+ phosphor is 0.81 eV, and multimodal optical information encoding devices were prepared by utilizing the difference between the different traps. Their fluorescence properties and optical information storage properties were investigated, and a series of flexible optical information storage films and high transmittance flexible optical information coding devices for optical data storage and information security were prepared using spin-coating and screen-printing techniques. This flexible PDMS luminescent layers showed a specific “National Water Conservation Label” storage mode by temperature stimulation response, which can erase and repeatedly read the information, a breakthrough for the first time to achieve more than 60 days of information storage. Fluorescence spectra of prepared Eu2+/Ln3+ double-doped samples showed that Ln3+ ions as trap centers in the phosphor structure and were able to modulate the trap energy level. The optical information encoder could read information in multiple modes. Optical information coding devices can be used to read information in multiple modes, solving the problem of single, insecure, and improving the practicality.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by the Science and Technology Innovation Leader and Key Talent Team Project of Shanxi Province (202204051002034) and Shanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Project (202102130501002).
